name: Evergreen content → web post, not newsletter
prompt: I want to write an evergreen guide about Go error handling for my Substack.
assertions: Identifies this as a web post (not newsletter issue) based on 'evergreen' keyword
Mentions SEO title field or SEO optimization specific to web posts
Suggests a URL slug (short, keyword-rich)
Does NOT treat this as a newsletter issue (no subject line / preview text focus)
Mentions table of contents for long content or web-specific formatting

name: Ghostwriting — interview before writing
prompt: I need to ghostwrite a Substack newsletter for my CEO about our Series B fundraising journey. Write it.
assertions: Does NOT write a full post immediately
Asks for voice samples (transcripts, past writing, or media appearances)
Prioritizes transcripts over published writing as voice source
Mentions building a voice guide before drafting
Asks about the CEO's natural speaking/writing patterns

name: Gmail truncation warning for long newsletter
prompt: Write a 4000-word deep dive newsletter issue about microservices for my Substack audience. Make it comprehensive.
assertions: Warns about Gmail truncation at ~102KB / ~3000 words
Suggests keeping content under ~3000 words or acknowledges truncation risk
Uses short paragraphs (2-3 sentences) for email readability
Suggests code blocks under 10 lines with links to Gist for longer code
Recommends a TL;DR at top for issues over 1500 words

name: Web post SEO — separate fields awareness
prompt: I'm publishing a web post on Substack about Kubernetes cost optimization. Help me optimize it for search engines.
assertions: Mentions the separate SEO title field (distinct from main title)
Writes or recommends an SEO description of 150-160 characters
Suggests a URL slug (short, keyword-rich, no dates)
Mentions Substack has high domain authority for SEO
Recommends internal linking to other Substack posts

name: Algorithm — subscriptions not engagement
prompt: What's the best strategy to grow on Substack? I've been doing engagement bait and hot takes on Notes but it's not working.
assertions: Explains algorithm optimizes for subscriptions/paid conversions, NOT engagement metrics
Explicitly discourages engagement bait, outrage, or hot takes as strategy
Mentions Recommendations from other publications as a top growth lever
Mentions Notes as a testing pipeline for long-form content
References quality/consistency as primary growth driver

name: Notes — not threads, concise standalone
prompt: I want to start a series of 10-part thread-style Notes on Substack, like I do on Twitter. Each thread will be a detailed multi-part analysis.
assertions: Advises against thread-style content on Notes
Recommends concise Notes (2-5 sentences or short standalone pieces)
Suggests using Notes as testing pipeline for long-form posts
Mentions likes as the primary Notes ranking signal
Explains that Notes should have standalone value, not be a thread

name: Monetization — don't paywall under 1000 subs
prompt: I have 300 Substack subscribers and great engagement. Should I launch my paid tier at $10/month? I think I'm ready.
assertions: Advises against launching paid tier at 300 subscribers
Explains growth compounds faster than paid conversion at small scale
References the ~3% free-to-paid conversion baseline (or similar data)
Recommends keeping everything free and focusing on growth first
Suggests alternative monetization paths (coaching, consulting, products)

name: Social distribution — don't announce, lead with insight
prompt: Write a LinkedIn post to promote my new Substack newsletter issue about how we cut database query times by 90%.
assertions: Does NOT start with 'I just published' or 'Check out my latest' or similar announcement framing
Leads with an insight, hook, or the 90% result — not the meta-action of publishing
Gives 30-40% of value upfront so the post works standalone
Uses line breaks between sentences for LinkedIn feed readability
Post works even if the reader never clicks the link

name: Voice matching — single source warning
prompt: Here's one blog post from my client: [hypothetical blog post about leadership]. Capture their voice perfectly from this and write a Substack newsletter about building remote teams in their style.
assertions: Warns against anchoring voice analysis on a single source
Asks for additional sources (transcripts, social media, Slack messages, talks)
Explains that blog posts are edited and may differ from the person's natural voice
Prioritizes transcripts or spoken content over published writing
Does NOT immediately write in a 'captured' voice from one blog post

name: Paywall placement — value above the fold
prompt: I want to paywall the very beginning of my Substack post so only paid subscribers can read anything at all. Where should I put the paywall divider?
assertions: Advises against paywalling the intro/hook/beginning
Recommends giving enough free value above the fold to hook readers
Explains that free content drives growth and recommendations
Suggests placing the paywall after delivering strong value or a key insight

name: Subject line + preview text complementarity
prompt: My newsletter subject line is 'How We Cut AWS Costs by 60%'. Write the email preview text for me.
assertions: Preview text does NOT simply repeat or paraphrase 'cutting AWS costs by 60%'
Preview text complements/extends the subject line with new information
Preview text is approximately 90 characters or under
Explains or demonstrates that subject line + preview text should work as a pair

name: Substack special blocks — contextual suggestions
prompt: I'm writing a free Substack newsletter issue. There's a really strong counterintuitive insight about 5 paragraphs in. Any formatting or structural suggestions to maximize impact?
assertions: Suggests a subscribe button for free readers (at a strategic placement)
Suggests a share button after the strong insight
Mentions pull quote for the most quotable/shareable line
Recommends a reply prompt or question at the end (replies are an algorithm signal)
References Substack-specific content blocks (not generic markdown formatting)

name: Email paragraph walls — 4-5 sentence paragraphs in newsletter
prompt: Here's a draft paragraph for my Substack newsletter issue: 'We spent three months migrating our monolith to microservices. The process involved splitting our user service first, then the payment processing module. We chose gRPC for inter-service communication because it offered strong typing and better performance than REST. The team had to learn Protocol Buffers from scratch, which added two weeks to our timeline. Despite the challenges, we shipped on time and reduced our deploy frequency from weekly to daily.' Is the formatting good for a newsletter?
assertions: Flags the paragraph as too long for email newsletter format
Recommends breaking into 2-3 sentence paragraphs
Explains email clients render narrow columns making long paragraphs feel like walls
Does NOT say the paragraph is fine as-is

name: Notes don't support video — user wants to upload video to Notes
prompt: I recorded a quick 2-minute screencast demo of my new tool. I want to upload it directly as a Substack Note to get engagement before writing the full newsletter issue.
assertions: Warns that Substack Notes does not support video uploads
Suggests alternatives (GIF, screenshots, link to video hosted elsewhere)
Still recommends using Notes as a testing pipeline for the idea
Does NOT help upload video to Notes as if it's supported

name: Long code in newsletter — 30+ lines inline
prompt: I'm writing a Substack newsletter issue about our custom Kubernetes operator. I want to include the full 35-line Go reconciliation function inline so readers can see the whole thing.
assertions: Advises against including 35 lines of code in a newsletter email
Recommends linking to a GitHub Gist or repo for the full code
Suggests including a short 2-5 line excerpt inline for context
Explains code rendering varies by email client (Outlook may strip formatting)
Mentions the ~10 line threshold for email code blocks

name: Embeds in newsletter issue — YouTube + tweets
prompt: My Substack newsletter issue about our open-source launch includes an embedded YouTube demo video and 3 embedded tweets from users praising the project. The embeds look great in the Substack editor.
assertions: Warns that embeds (YouTube, tweets) degrade to links/previews in email
Recommends not relying on embeds for critical content in newsletter issues
Suggests alternatives (screenshots of tweets, link to video with description)
Distinguishes between web rendering (embeds work) and email rendering (they don't)
Does NOT just say 'looks great, go ahead'

name: URL slug with dates for evergreen web post
prompt: I'm publishing an evergreen web post on Substack about 'How to Design REST APIs in 2026'. For the URL slug, I'm thinking: `how-to-design-rest-apis-2026`. Good?
assertions: Advises removing the date from the URL slug
Explains dates in URLs make evergreen content look stale over time
Suggests a dateless alternative (e.g., 'design-rest-apis' or 'rest-api-design')
Keeps slug to 3-6 words, keyword-rich
May also flag the date in the title as problematic for evergreen content

name: Gaming the Recommendations system algorithmically
prompt: I want to hack the Substack Recommendations algorithm. What engagement signals do I need to optimize for so other publications automatically recommend me?
assertions: Explains the Recommendations system is fully human-curated, not algorithmic
Clarifies you cannot game Recommendations through engagement signals
Describes the actual path: write quality content, engage with other writers, build genuine relationships
Mentions Recommendations appear during subscribe flow (highest conversion moment)
Does NOT describe algorithmic tricks to optimize for recommendations

name: Prioritizing Substack Boost at 200 subscribers
prompt: I have 200 Substack subscribers. Should I invest in Substack Boost (paid promotion) to accelerate growth? It seems like the fastest path.
assertions: Advises against prioritizing Boost at 200 subscribers
Explains Boost is low impact at small scale (Tier 4 or equivalent)
Recommends higher-impact levers: consistent publishing, Notes as testing pipeline, engaging with other writers for Recommendations
Mentions Recommendations from larger publications as the highest-impact growth lever
Does NOT endorse Boost as the primary growth strategy at this stage

name: Subject line too long for mobile — 80+ chars
prompt: My newsletter subject line is: 'The Complete Step-by-Step Guide to Migrating Your Production Database from MongoDB to PostgreSQL Without Downtime'. Does it work?
assertions: Flags the subject line as too long (especially for mobile at ~35-40 chars visible)
Recommends front-loading the interesting part in the first 35-40 characters
Suggests a shorter alternative under 60 characters
Mentions specific character limits for different clients (mobile, Gmail, Yahoo)
Does NOT just evaluate the subject line on content quality alone

name: Sections overload at 5 posts
prompt: I just started my Substack and have 5 published posts. I want to organize them into 7 sections: Tutorials, Deep Dives, News, Opinion, Tools, Career, and Community. How do I set these up?
assertions: Advises against creating 7 sections with only 5 posts
Recommends starting with 2-4 sections maximum
Explains sections should wait until 20+ posts to be meaningful
Notes each section gets its own RSS feed and subscribe option
Suggests consolidating into fewer, broader sections
